title: American 4-4-0
body: Long before cars or planes, fire and steam moved the world. The American 4-4-0 was the workhorse of the rails, racing iron and timber across a young United States.

title: Saddle Tank Locomotive
body: Across the ocean, smaller engines like the 0-4-0 Saddle Tank puffed through factories and ports. Open it up and you find a tiny world inside: firebox, boiler, pistons.

title: The V8 Engine
body: While trains still ruled the rails, inventors were dreaming of horse-less carriages. The V8 — eight cylinders firing in turn — gave cars the heart they needed to fly down a road.

title: Oliver 70
body: On the farm, horses were giving way to iron. The Oliver 70 turned plowing into something a single farmer could do all morning. Two ways to see it: whole, or in pieces.

title: Focke-Wulf Fw 190
body: By the time tractors were transforming fields, fighter planes filled the skies. The Fw 190 was fast, agile, and feared — one of the most advanced aircraft of the Second World War.

title: Tiger I
body: On the ground at the same time, the Tiger I rolled into battle. Heavy armor, a huge cannon — it looked unstoppable. But every machine has a weakness, and the war taught hard lessons.

title: M26 Pershing
body: Three years later, the M26 Pershing answered. Bigger gun, thicker armor — built to face the Tiger head-on. By the time it arrived, the war was almost over.

title: Dodge Power Wagon
body: Peace came, and the trucks of war found a new job. The Dodge Power Wagon swapped the battlefield for the farm, hauling hay, mud, and weekend adventures.

title: Ferguson TE20
body: That same year, the little gray Fergie quietly changed farming forever. Inside, a small engine that's not so different from a car's — but bolted to wheels made to dig in and pull.

title: Chevy Advance Design
body: Right next to the Power Wagon, Chevy rolled out a softer-looking pickup with a curvier hood. Same job, gentler style. The American driveway had a new favorite.

title: Cessna 172
body: A decade later, planes shrank to fit small airports and student pilots. The Cessna 172 became the most-flown training aircraft ever built — many pilots learn the sky in one.

title: VW Fusca
body: Volkswagen started building the Beetle in São Bernardo do Campo this year — the people's car finally arrived in Brazil. Air-cooled engine in the back, round headlights up front, a curve you'd recognize anywhere.

title: Hydraulic Excavator
body: On construction sites everywhere, the hydraulic excavator took over the digging. Its arm could lift, push, and scoop — driven by oil pressure pumped through fat hoses.

title: Excavator on Tracks
body: Tracks instead of wheels meant work could happen in mud, sand, even on snow. Excavators became the bulldogs of the worksite — slow, heavy, and unstoppable.

title: ZIL-131
body: On the other side of the world, the ZIL-131 hauled almost anything across Soviet roads — and where there were no roads, it made its own.

title: Sikorsky CH-53
body: Up above, helicopters joined the family. The CH-53 Sea Stallion could carry tanks slung from below — a flying truck for places no road could reach.

title: Mercedes-Benz LO 1113
body: On Brazilian roads, a different machine was carrying everyone — the LO 1113. People called it the cara-de-cavalo, the horse-face, for its rounded snout and tall headlights. A workhorse that climbed mountains, crossed states, ran for decades.

title: The Jet Engine
body: Open up an airplane's engine and you find this: blades spinning faster than your eyes can follow, sucking in air, mixing it with fuel, lighting it up. Out the back comes thrust — and the plane goes forward.

title: Boeing 747
body: The same year humans walked on the Moon, the Boeing 747 took off — the first jumbo jet. Suddenly airplanes carried 400 people at a time, and the world felt smaller.

title: Crawler Crane
body: Down below, cities were growing taller. The crawler crane crept onto building sites on giant treads, lifting steel beams a hundred meters into the sky.

title: T-150 Tractor
body: Back on the farm, tractors had grown enormous. The Soviet T-150 had four-wheel drive and an articulated body that bent in the middle — built to plow endless Russian fields.

title: Fire Truck
body: Every fire truck is a tiny city of tools. Ladder, water tank, pump, hoses, helmets, axes — open one up like this and you can spot a job for every part.

title: Dodge Diplomat
body: On American streets in the 80s, the boxy Dodge Diplomat became the classic police cruiser. Big V8, big trunk, big lights on top — the unmistakable shape of the era.

title: Harley Heritage Softail
body: Motorcycles got nostalgic. The Harley-Davidson Heritage Softail looked like it stepped out of 1950 but rode like a modern machine. Old soul, fresh suspension.

title: Ural Fire Truck
body: That same year, in a small town called Pripyat, Ural fire trucks raced toward a reactor that wouldn't stop burning. Some machines remember the most important nights.

title: Harley Road King
body: By the 90s, the touring motorcycle had become its own kind of luxury. The Road King carried riders coast to coast — chrome, leather, and the open highway.

title: Chevy Silverado
body: Pickup trucks kept getting bigger and more comfortable. The Silverado became one of America's best-selling vehicles — a workhorse that also doubled as the family car.

title: Shinkansen N700
body: Trains, too, had grown up. The Japanese Shinkansen N700 glides across the country at 300 km/h — silent, smooth, almost flying. Steam, never go away. We just got better at it.

title: Liebherr LR 13000
body: The biggest crane ever built on caterpillar tracks. The LR 13000 can lift the weight of 5 fully loaded Boeing 747s. Skyscrapers and wind turbines wouldn't exist without it.

title: CRRC CR450 Fuxing
body: Steam started this story. The CR450 is where it goes now — the fastest commercial train in the world at 450 km/h. The needle nose slices through air so fast the wind barely notices it passing.

title: See You Soon
body: From the first steam engine to the tallest crane, machines keep changing. But there's always a kid somewhere with a book, wanting to know how things work. That's where every new machine begins.
